7: ---
8: 
9: <!-- Updated: 2026-04-13 | v1.5 -->
10: 
11: # Competitor Ad Intelligence
12: 
13: ## Process
14: 
15: 1. Identify target competitors (from user input or industry analysis)
16: 2. Read `ads/references/benchmarks.md` for industry CPC/CTR/CVR baselines
17: 3. Research competitor ad presence across platforms
18: 4. Analyze ad copy, creative, and messaging themes
19: 5. Estimate competitor spend and keyword strategy
20: 6. Identify gaps and opportunities
21: 7. Generate competitive intelligence report
22: 
23: ## Data Sources
24: 
25: ### Free Intelligence Sources
26: | Source | Platform | What You Can Find |
27: |--------|----------|------------------|
28: | Google Ads Transparency Center | Google | Active ads, formats, geo targeting |
29: | Meta Ad Library | Meta/Instagram | All active ads, creative, copy, spend range |
30: | LinkedIn Ad Library | LinkedIn | Active ads from company pages |
31: | TikTok Creative Center | TikTok | Top ads, trending creative, hashtags |
32: | Microsoft Ads | Microsoft | Limited: use auction insights |
33: | Apple Ads (App Store) | Apple | Search tab ads, Today tab ads, product page creatives |
34: 
35: ### Google Ads Auction Insights
36: Available from the user's own Google Ads account:
37: - Impression share vs competitors
38: - Overlap rate (how often you compete)
39: - Outranking share (who wins more often)
40: - Top of page rate and absolute top of page rate
41: - Available for Search and Shopping campaigns
42: 
43: ### Platform-Specific Research
44: 
45: #### Google
46: - Ads Transparency Center: search by advertiser name or domain
47: - Search for competitor brand terms to see their ads live
48: - Auction Insights for impression share comparison
49: 
50: #### Meta
51: - Ad Library: filter by advertiser, country, platform (FB/IG), date range
52: - Shows creative (image/video), ad copy, active dates
53: - Shows platform placement (Facebook, Instagram, Audience Network)
54: 
55: #### LinkedIn
56: - Ad Library: search by company name
57: - Shows Sponsored Content, Message Ads
58: - Limited data compared to Meta Ad Library
59: 
60: #### TikTok
61: - Creative Center 2.0: top-performing ads by industry, country, objective with enhanced filtering
62: - Hashtag analytics: trending sounds and hashtags
63: - TikTok Shop: review competitor storefronts, catalogs, reviews, and live shopping
64: - No per-advertiser library; use Creative Center for industry trends
65: 
66: #### Apple Ads
67: - App Store search: search competitor brand terms to see their Search tab ads
68: - Today tab ads: check competitor presence on App Store homepage
69: - Custom Product Pages: analyze competitor CPP variants via App Store listings
70: 
71: ## 2025-2026 Platform Updates
72: 
73: ### Meta
74: - **Andromeda creative clustering** (launched October 2025): ads with >60% similarity are suppressed. Competitor creative analysis must assess genuine conceptual diversity, not just volume
75: - **Advantage+ Sales** (renamed from ASC): unified shopping campaign type. Check if competitors are running Advantage+ Sales vs manual campaigns
76: 
77: ### Google
78: - **Demand Gen replaced VAC** (April 2026): Video Action Campaigns fully deprecated. Analyze competitor Demand Gen adoption (multi-format video+image)
79: - **AI Max for Search**: AI-powered campaign type with 14% conversion lift. Check competitor adoption via Ads Transparency Center
80: - **Expanded Ads Transparency Center**: richer data on competitor ad history, formats, and targeting regions
81: 
82: ### TikTok
83: - **Creative Center 2.0**: enhanced industry benchmarking, top ads filtering by objective and format
84: - **Symphony AI variations** (2025): AI-generated creative at scale. Assess whether competitors use Symphony-generated vs original content
85: - **TikTok Shop competitor analysis**: review competitor Shop tabs, product catalogs, reviews, and live shopping activity
86: 
87: ### LinkedIn
88: - **Thought Leader Ads expansion** (March 2025): now supports non-employee creators and influencers. Check if competitors sponsor external thought leaders
89: - **CRM integration** (June 2025): enhanced audience matching. Competitors with CRM integration show more precise targeting patterns
90: 
91: ### Microsoft
92: - **Copilot ads in conversations**: new placement in Microsoft Copilot chat. Check if competitors appear in conversational ad placements
93: - **CTV inventory**: ads on Netflix, Max, Hulu, and Roku via Microsoft Advertising. Assess competitor presence on connected TV
94: 
95: ### Apple Ads
96: - **Rebranded to "Apple Ads"** (April 2025): formerly Apple Search Ads. All references updated
97: - **Custom Product Page (CPP) competitive analysis**: competitors can run different product page variants per ad group. Analyze CPP strategies
98: - **Maximize Conversions bidding**: AI auto-bidder (GA February 2026). Check if competitors have adopted automated bidding
99: 
100: > **MCP Integration**: For live API access to competitor data sources, see `ads/references/mcp-integration.md`.
101: 
102: ## Competitive Analysis Framework
103: 
104: ### 1. Ad Copy Analysis
105: For each competitor, document:
106: - **Headlines**: primary messages and value propositions
107: - **CTAs**: what action they're driving (free trial, demo, buy now, learn more)
108: - **Offers**: pricing, discounts, free shipping, trials
109: - **Tone**: professional, casual, urgent, educational, emotional
110: - **USPs**: unique selling propositions they emphasize
111: - **Pain points**: customer problems they address
112: 
113: ### 2. Creative Strategy Analysis
114: - **Formats used**: image, video, carousel, collection, document
115: - **Visual style**: photography, illustration, UGC, stock, branded
116: - **Video approach**: studio quality vs UGC vs animated
117: - **Creative volume**: how many active ads (indicator of testing velocity)
118: - **Refresh frequency**: how often new creatives appear
119: 
120: ### 3. Messaging Themes
121: Categorize competitor messaging into themes:
122: | Theme | Competitor A | Competitor B | Your Brand |
123: |-------|-------------|-------------|------------|
124: | Price/Value | ✅ Primary | ⚠️ Secondary | ? |
125: | Quality/Premium | ❌ | ✅ Primary | ? |
126: | Speed/Convenience | ⚠️ Secondary | ❌ | ? |
127: | Trust/Authority | ✅ Primary | ✅ Primary | ? |
128: | Innovation | ❌ | ⚠️ Secondary | ? |
129: 
130: ### 4. Keyword Intelligence (Google/Microsoft/Apple Ads)
131: - Brand keyword bidding: are competitors bidding on your brand?
132: - Keyword overlap: which non-brand terms do you both target?
133: - Keyword gaps: terms competitors rank for that you don't target
134: - Match type strategy: estimated match types from ad triggers
135: 
136: ### 5. Spend Estimation
137: - Meta Ad Library shows spend ranges for political/social ads
138: - Google Auction Insights + impression share = directional spend estimate
139: - Third-party tools (SEMrush, SpyFu) for more precise estimates
140: - Manual estimation formula:
141:   ```
142:   Estimated Monthly Spend = Impressions × CPM / 1000
143:   or
144:   Estimated Monthly Spend = Clicks × Estimated CPC
145:   ```
146: 
147: ## Gap & Opportunity Identification
148: 
149: ### Platform Gaps
150: - Which platforms are competitors NOT on? (opportunity to own)
151: - Which platforms are they underspending on? (opportunity to outspend)
152: 
153: ### Messaging Gaps
154: - What customer pain points are NO competitors addressing?
155: - What value propositions are underrepresented in the market?
156: - What content formats are competitors not using?
157: 
158: ### Audience Gaps
159: - What demographics/segments are competitors not targeting?
160: - What geographic markets are underserved?
161: - What funnel stages are competitors neglecting?
162: 
163: ### Creative Gaps
164: - What ad formats are competitors not using? (video, UGC, Spark Ads)
165: - What creative styles are missing from the competitive landscape?
166: - What platform-specific features are competitors not leveraging?
167: 
168: ## Competitive Response Strategy
169: 
170: ### When Competitors Bid on Your Brand
171: - Always run brand campaigns to defend (low CPC, high CTR)
172: - Dynamic keyword insertion to show your brand prominently
173: - Sitelinks to key pages (pricing, features, reviews)
174: - Ad copy that emphasizes unique differentiators
175: - Consider bidding on competitor brand terms (know the rules)
176: 
177: ### When You're Outspent
178: - Focus on efficiency over volume (better targeting, creative, landing pages)
179: - Target long-tail keywords competitors ignore
180: - Use Exact match for precision (less waste)
181: - Double down on retargeting (lower CPA than prospecting)
182: - Compete on creative quality, not budget
183: 
184: ## Output
185: 
186: ### Deliverables
187: - `COMPETITOR-INTELLIGENCE-REPORT.md`: Full competitive analysis
188:   - Per-competitor ad presence summary
189:   - Ad copy and messaging analysis
190:   - Creative strategy comparison
191:   - Estimated spend levels
192:   - Keyword overlap and gaps
193: - `COMPETITIVE-GAPS.md`: Opportunities identified from competitor analysis
194:   - Platform gaps
195:   - Messaging opportunities
196:   - Audience segments to target
197:   - Creative format opportunities
198: - Strategic recommendations for competitive positioning
199: - Priority actions to gain competitive advantage

(End of file - total 199 lines)
</content>

7: ---
8: 
9: # PPC Financial Calculator & Modeling
10: 
11: <!-- Created: 2026-04-13 | v1.5 -->
12: <!-- Source: itallstartedwithaidea/google-ads-skills (PPC math concept) -->
13: 
14: ## Process
15: 
16: 1. Ask the user what calculation they need (or detect from context)
17: 2. Collect required inputs (from pasted data, exports, or verbal description)
18: 3. Perform calculations with clear formulas shown
19: 4. Present results with interpretation and recommendations
20: 5. Flag any concerning metrics or benchmarks
21: 
22: ## Calculators
23: 
24: ### 1. CPA Calculator
25: 
26: ```
27: CPA = Total Spend / Total Conversions
28: 
29: Inputs needed:
30: - Total ad spend (period)
31: - Total conversions (same period)
32: 
33: Output:
34: - CPA with period context
35: - CPA trend (if historical data provided)
36: - Comparison to industry benchmark (from benchmarks.md)
37: ```
38: 
39: ### 2. ROAS Calculator
40: 
41: ```
42: ROAS = Revenue from Ads / Ad Spend
43: ROAS% = (Revenue - Spend) / Spend × 100
44: 
45: Inputs needed:
46: - Total ad spend
47: - Total revenue attributed to ads
48: 
49: Output:
50: - ROAS as ratio (e.g., 3.5x) and percentage (250%)
51: - Break-even ROAS (based on margins if provided)
52: - Comparison to platform benchmarks
53: ```
54: 
55: ### 3. Break-Even Analysis
56: 
57: ```
58: Break-Even CPA = Average Order Value × Profit Margin
59: Break-Even ROAS = 1 / Profit Margin
60: 
61: Inputs needed:
62: - Average order value (AOV) OR average deal value
63: - Profit margin (gross margin %)
64: - Current CPA or ROAS
65: 
66: Output:
67: - Maximum profitable CPA
68: - Minimum profitable ROAS
69: - Current headroom (how far above/below break-even)
70: - Recommendation: scale, maintain, or cut
71: ```
72: 
73: ### 4. Impression Share Opportunity
74: 
75: ```
76: Impression Share Lost (Budget) = opportunity from budget increase
77: Impression Share Lost (Rank) = opportunity from bid/quality improvement
78: 
79: Revenue Opportunity = Current Revenue × (1 / Current IS - 1)
80: 
81: Inputs needed:
82: - Current impression share %
83: - IS lost to budget %
84: - IS lost to rank %
85: - Current spend and conversions
86: 
87: Output:
88: - Estimated additional conversions from full IS
89: - Budget needed for full IS (estimated)
90: - Priority: budget increase vs quality improvement
91: ```
92: 
93: ### 5. Budget Forecasting
94: 
95: ```
96: Projected Spend = Daily Budget × Days in Period
97: Projected Conversions = Projected Spend / Historical CPA
98: Projected Revenue = Projected Conversions × AOV
99: 
100: Scaling scenarios:
101: - Conservative: +20% budget → estimated impact
102: - Moderate: +50% budget → estimated impact
103: - Aggressive: +100% budget → estimated impact (with diminishing returns caveat)
104: 
105: Inputs needed:
106: - Current daily budget
107: - Historical CPA (last 30 days)
108: - Forecast period
109: - AOV (if revenue projection needed)
110: 
111: Output:
112: - 3 scenarios with spend, conversions, revenue projections
113: - Diminishing returns warning for aggressive scaling
114: - 20% scaling rule reminder (never increase >20% at a time)
115: ```
116: 
117: ### 6. LTV:CAC Ratio
118: 
119: ```
120: CAC = Total Marketing Spend / New Customers Acquired
121: LTV = Average Revenue per Customer × Average Customer Lifespan
122: LTV:CAC Ratio = LTV / CAC
123: 
124: Inputs needed:
125: - Total marketing spend (all channels)
126: - New customers acquired
127: - Average revenue per customer (monthly or annual)
128: - Average customer lifespan (months)
129: - Optional: gross margin for unit economics
130: 
131: Output:
132: - LTV:CAC ratio with interpretation:
133:   - <1:1 = losing money on every customer
134:   - 1:1-2:1 = break-even to marginal
135:   - 3:1 = healthy (SaaS benchmark)
136:   - 5:1+ = may be under-investing in growth
137: - Payback period: months to recover CAC
138: - Recommendation based on ratio
139: ```
140: 
141: ### 7. MER (Marketing Efficiency Ratio)
142: 
143: ```
144: MER = Total Revenue / Total Marketing Spend
145: 
146: Inputs needed:
147: - Total business revenue (period)
148: - Total marketing spend across ALL channels (same period)
149: 
150: Output:
151: - MER ratio (e.g., 5.0x)
152: - Interpretation:
153:   - E-commerce: 3-5x typical, 8x+ excellent
154:   - SaaS: 5-10x typical (higher margins)
155:   - Local service: 3-8x typical
156: - Comparison to business-type benchmark
157: - Note: MER captures blended efficiency including organic, brand, and retention
158: ```
159: 
160: ### Incrementality & Advanced Measurement
161: 
162: For advanced accounts evaluating cross-channel contribution:
163: - **Meta Incremental Attribution** (launched April 2025): AI-powered holdout testing measuring real causal impact. Evaluate if budget exceeds $5K/month.
164: - **Google Meridian** (2025): Open-source Marketing Mix Model for incrementality measurement across channels.
165: - These tools complement PPC math calculations by measuring what would NOT have happened without the ad spend.
166: 
167: For large accounts detecting small effects (5% MDE), multiply the 10% MDE sample by ~4x.
168: 
169: ## Quick Formulas Reference
170: 
171: | Metric | Formula |
172: |--------|---------|
173: | CPA | Spend / Conversions |
174: | ROAS | Revenue / Spend |
175: | CTR | Clicks / Impressions × 100 |
176: | CVR | Conversions / Clicks × 100 |
177: | CPC | Spend / Clicks |
178: | CPM | (Spend / Impressions) × 1,000 |
179: | CPL | Spend / Leads |
180: | Break-Even CPA | AOV × Margin% |
181: | Break-Even ROAS | 1 / Margin% |
182: | LTV | ARPU × Avg Lifespan |
183: | CAC | Total Marketing / New Customers |
184: | MER | Total Revenue / Total Marketing |
185: | Impression Share Opp | Revenue × (1/IS - 1) |
186: 
187: ## Output Format
188: 
189: ```
190: ## PPC Financial Analysis
191: 
192: ### [Calculator Name]
193: 
194: **Inputs:**
195: - [Listed inputs with values]
196: 
197: **Results:**
198: | Metric | Value | Benchmark | Status |
199: |--------|-------|-----------|--------|
200: | [Metric] | [Value] | [Benchmark] | PASS/WARNING/FAIL |
201: 
202: **Interpretation:**
203: [1-2 sentence analysis]
204: 
205: **Recommendation:**
206: [Actionable next step]
207: ```
208: 
209: ## Data to Request
210: 
211: If the user doesn't provide enough data, ask for:
212: - Platform and campaign type
213: - Time period for analysis
214: - Spend and conversion data
215: - Revenue data (if ROAS/break-even needed)
216: - Margin data (if break-even/LTV needed)
217: - Business type (for benchmark comparison)

(End of file - total 217 lines)
</content>

2: name: ads-test

Structured hypothesis framework, statistical significance calculator, test duration estimator, sample size calculator, and platform-specific experiment setup guides (Meta Experiments, Google Experiments, LinkedIn A/B). Use when user says A/B test, split test, experiment design, test hypothesis, statistical significance, sample size, or test duration."
4: user-invokable: false

7: ---
8: 
9: # A/B Test Design & Experiment Planning
10: 
11: <!-- Created: 2026-04-13 | v1.5 -->
12: <!-- Source: OpenClaudia/openclaudia-skills (ab-test-setup concept) -->
13: 
14: ## Process
15: 
16: 1. Understand what the user wants to test (creative, audience, bidding, landing page)
17: 2. Build structured hypothesis using the framework below
18: 3. Calculate required sample size and estimated duration
19: 4. Recommend platform-specific test setup
20: 5. Define success criteria and measurement plan
21: 
22: ## Hypothesis Framework
23: 
24: Every test must start with a structured hypothesis:
25: 
26: ```
27: IF we [change/action]
28: THEN [metric] will [increase/decrease] by [estimated %]
29: BECAUSE [reasoning based on data or insight]
30: 
31: Example:
32: IF we replace polished product shots with UGC creator videos
33: THEN Meta CTR will increase by 25-40%
34: BECAUSE Andromeda prioritizes diverse creative formats and UGC consistently outperforms polished in 2025-2026 benchmarks
35: ```
36: 
37: ### Hypothesis Quality Checklist
38: - [ ] Single variable being tested (isolate the change)
39: - [ ] Specific metric defined (not "performance")
40: - [ ] Estimated effect size stated (needed for sample size calculation)
41: - [ ] Timeframe defined
42: - [ ] Success/failure criteria clear before launch
43: 
44: ## Statistical Significance Calculator
45: 
46: ```
47: Required Sample Size (per variant):
48: 
49: n = (Z_alpha + Z_beta)^2 × 2 × p × (1-p) / MDE^2
50: 
51: Where:
52: - Z_alpha = 1.96 (for 95% confidence)
53: - Z_beta = 0.84 (for 80% power)
54: - p = baseline conversion rate
55: - MDE = minimum detectable effect (relative %)
56: 
57: Simplified lookup:
58: ```
59: 
60: | Baseline CVR | 5% MDE | 10% MDE | 20% MDE | 30% MDE |
61: |-------------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
62: | 1% | 612,000 | 153,000 | 38,300 | 17,000 |
63: | 2% | 302,400 | 75,600 | 18,900 | 8,400 |
64: | 5% | 116,800 | 29,200 | 7,300 | 3,200 |
65: | 10% | 55,200 | 13,800 | 3,450 | 1,530 |
66: | 20% | 24,600 | 6,150 | 1,540 | 680 |
67: 
68: *Per variant, 95% confidence, 80% power*
69: 
70: ## Test Duration Estimator
71: 
72: ```
73: Duration = Required Sample Size / Daily Traffic per Variant
74: 
75: Minimum duration: 7 days (capture weekly patterns)
76: Maximum recommended: 28 days (avoid seasonal drift)
77: Learning phase: Google 7-14 days, Meta 3-7 days, LinkedIn 7-14 days
78: 
79: Inputs needed:
80: - Daily impressions or clicks
81: - Number of variants (2 = A/B, 3+ = multivariate)
82: - Baseline conversion rate
83: - Minimum detectable effect desired
84: ```
85: 
86: ### Duration Quick Estimates
87: 
88: | Daily Clicks | 2% CVR, 20% MDE | 5% CVR, 20% MDE | 10% CVR, 20% MDE |
89: |-------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
90: | 100 | 189 days | 73 days | 35 days |
91: | 500 | 38 days | 15 days | 7 days |
92: | 1,000 | 19 days | 7 days | 4 days* |
93: | 5,000 | 4 days* | 2 days* | 1 day* |
94: 
95: *Minimum 7 days recommended regardless of sample sufficiency
96: 
97: ## Platform-Specific Test Setup
98: 
99: ### Meta Experiments
100: - Use Ads Manager > Experiments tab (not manual ad set duplication)
101: - Automatic audience splitting ensures no overlap
102: - Supported test types: A/B (creative, audience, placement), Holdout, Brand Survey
103: - Meta's Incremental Attribution (April 2025) provides AI-powered holdout testing for measuring real causal impact
104: - Budget: split evenly across variants; minimum $100/day per variant recommended
105: - Duration: 7-14 days typical; Meta auto-determines winner at 95% confidence
106: 
107: ### Google Experiments
108: - Campaign Experiments (custom experiments) or Ad Variations
109: - Create experiment from existing campaign > select experiment type
110: - Traffic split: 50/50 recommended for fastest results
111: - Supported: bidding strategy, ad copy, landing page, audience
112: - Metrics: choose primary metric (conversions, CPA, ROAS) before launch
113: - Duration: 14-30 days recommended; minimum 2 weeks for bidding tests
114: 
115: ### LinkedIn A/B Testing
116: - Built into Campaign Manager for Sponsored Content
117: - Duplicate ad set with single variable change
118: - Target: same audience segment with automatic rotation
119: - Minimum budget: $50/day per variant
120: - Key metrics: CTR (>0.44% benchmark), CPL, Lead Form CVR (13% benchmark)
121: - Duration: 14-21 days (LinkedIn's smaller daily volumes require longer tests)
122: 
123: ### TikTok Split Testing
124: - Available in TikTok Ads Manager > Create A/B Test
125: - Test types: targeting, bidding, creative
126: - Auto-splits audience to avoid contamination
127: - Minimum 7 days, recommended 14 days
128: - Budget: minimum $20/day per ad group
129: - Creative tests: isolate hook (first 2-3 seconds) as the primary variable
130: - TikTok's enhanced split testing supports modular test variables (targeting, creative, budget, placement) via Smart+ since 2025
131: 
132: ## What to Test (Priority Order)
133: 
134: ### High Impact (test first)
135: 1. **Creative concept** (different messaging angles, not just color changes)
136: 2. **Hook/first 3 seconds** (video opening on Meta, TikTok, YouTube)
137: 3. **Offer structure** (pricing, discount type, free trial length)
138: 4. **Landing page** (headline, CTA, form length)
139: 5. **Bidding strategy** (tCPA vs tROAS vs Maximize Conversions)
140: 
141: ### Medium Impact
142: 6. **Audience targeting** (interest vs lookalike vs broad)
143: 7. **Ad format** (static vs video vs carousel)
144: 8. **CTA button** (Learn More vs Sign Up vs Shop Now)
145: 9. **Campaign structure** (CBO vs ABO, consolidated vs segmented)
146: 
147: ### Low Impact (test last)
148: 10. **Ad scheduling** (time of day, day of week)
149: 11. **Device targeting** (mobile vs desktop)
150: 12. **Minor copy variations** (word substitutions without concept change)
151: 
152: ## Common Testing Mistakes to Avoid
153: 
154: - Testing too many variables at once (no clear winner attribution)
155: - Ending tests too early (before statistical significance)
156: - Testing during atypical periods (holidays, launches, incidents)
157: - Comparing unequal time periods
158: - Not documenting learnings (build institutional knowledge)
159: - Testing small changes when big changes are needed (optimize vs innovate)
160: - Ignoring learning phase on automated platforms
161: 
162: ## Output Format
163: 
164: ```
165: ## A/B Test Plan
166: 
167: ### Hypothesis
168: IF [change]
169: THEN [metric] will [direction] by [amount]
170: BECAUSE [reasoning]
171: 
172: ### Test Design
173: | Parameter | Value |
174: |-----------|-------|
175: | Platform | [platform] |
176: | Test Type | [A/B / Multivariate] |
177: | Variable | [what's being changed] |
178: | Control | [current state] |
179: | Variant | [proposed change] |
180: | Primary Metric | [KPI] |
181: | Traffic Split | [50/50 / other] |
182: 
183: ### Sample Size & Duration
184: | Metric | Value |
185: |--------|-------|
186: | Baseline CVR | [X%] |
187: | MDE | [X%] |
188: | Required Sample | [N per variant] |
189: | Daily Traffic | [N clicks/day] |
190: | Est. Duration | [X days] |
191: | Min Duration | 7 days |
192: 
193: ### Success Criteria
194: - Winner declared at 95% confidence
195: - [Primary metric] improvement of [X%]+ sustained over [Y] days
196: - No negative impact on [secondary metric]
197: 
198: ### Setup Instructions
199: [Platform-specific step-by-step]
200: ```

(End of file - total 200 lines)
</content>

1: ---
2: name: ads-dna
3: description: "Brand DNA extractor for paid advertising. Scans a website URL to extract visual identity, tone of voice, color palette, typography, and imagery style. Outputs brand-profile.json to the current directory. Run before /ads create or /ads generate for brand-consistent creative. Triggers on: brand DNA, brand profile, extract brand, brand identity, brand colors, what is the brand voice, analyze brand, brand style guide."

7: ---
8: 
9: # Ads DNA: Brand DNA Extractor
10: 
11: Extracts brand identity from a website and saves it as `brand-profile.json`
12: for use by `/ads create`, `/ads generate`, and `/ads photoshoot`.
13: 
14: Brand DNA extraction is OBSERVE and LISTEN made concrete (see the
15: **10-Principle Thinking Framework** in `ads/references/thinking-framework.md`).
16: The website is the input; the brand profile is what the site is *saying*
17: about itself when no one is curating the message. Listen to the voice
18: before you label it. Observe the visual choices before you classify them.
19: 
20: ## Quick Reference
21: 
22: | Command | What it does |
23: |---------|-------------|
24: | `/ads dna <url>` | Full brand extraction → `brand-profile.json` |
25: | `/ads dna https://acme.com --quick` | Fast extraction (homepage only) |
26: 
27: ## Process
28: 
29: ### Step 1: Collect URL
30: 
31: If the user hasn't provided a URL, ask:
32: > "What website URL should I analyze for brand DNA? (e.g. https://yoursite.com)"
33: 
34: ### Step 2: Fetch Pages
35: 
36: Use the **WebFetch tool** to retrieve each page. For each URL, use this fetch prompt:
37: > "Return all visible text content, the full contents of any `<style>` blocks, inline
38: > `style=` attributes, `<meta>` tags, Google Fonts `@import` URLs, and any `og:image`
39: > values found on this page."
40: 
41: Fetch in this order:
42: 1. **Homepage** (`<url>`)
43: 2. **About page**: try `<url>/about`, then `<url>/about-us`, then `<url>/our-story`
44: 3. **Product/Services page**: try `<url>/product`, then `<url>/products`, then `<url>/services`
45: 
46: **If `--quick` flag was provided**: fetch the homepage only; skip steps 2 and 3.
47: 
48: If a secondary page returns a 404 or redirect error, continue with fewer pages and note:
49: "Secondary pages unavailable; extraction based on homepage only. Confidence may be lower."
50: 
51: ### Step 2b: Capture Brand Screenshots
52: 
53: After fetching pages, capture 3 screenshots for comprehensive brand anchoring.
54: These serve as visual style references during `/ads generate`; the same approach
55: Pomelli uses to anchor ad images to the actual brand aesthetic.
56: 
57: Capture the following:
58: 
59: 1. **Homepage hero section** (above the fold):
60: ```bash
61: python ~/.claude/skills/ads/scripts/capture_screenshot.py [url]
62: ```
63: Saves: `./brand-screenshots/{domain}_homepage.png`
64: 
65: 2. **Product or services page**:
66: ```bash
67: python ~/.claude/skills/ads/scripts/capture_screenshot.py [url]/products
68: ```
69: Saves: `./brand-screenshots/{domain}_product.png`
70: 
71: 3. **About page** (brand personality):
72: ```bash
73: python ~/.claude/skills/ads/scripts/capture_screenshot.py [url]/about
74: ```
75: Saves: `./brand-screenshots/{domain}_about.png`
76: 
77: If a page is not found or returns an error, skip it gracefully and continue
78: with the remaining pages.
79: 
80: **If `--quick` flag was provided**: skip screenshot capture entirely.
81: 
82: **If capture fails** (Playwright not installed, network error, JS-heavy SPA that times out):
83: - Log: `"Screenshot capture skipped; run: python3 -m playwright install chromium"`
84: - Continue without screenshots
85: - Do NOT set the `screenshots` field in brand-profile.json
86: 
87: ### Step 3: Extract Brand Elements
88: 
89: From the fetched HTML, extract:
90: 
91: **Colors:**
92: - `og:image` meta tag → analyze dominant colors (note 2-3 prominent hex values)
93: - CSS `background-color` on `body`, `header`, `.hero`, `.btn-primary`
94: - CSS `color` on `h1`, `h2`, `.btn`
95: - CSS `border-color` or `background` on `.cta`, `.button`
96: - Identify: primary (most prominent brand color), secondary (supporting colors), background, text
97: 
98: **Typography:**
99: - `@import url(https://fonts.googleapis.com/...)` → extract font names from URL path
100: - CSS `font-family` on `h1`, `h2`, `body`, `.headline`
101: - If Google Fonts URL contains `family=Inter:wght@...`, heading_font = "Inter"
102: 
103: **Voice:**
104: Analyze hero headline, subheadline, About page intro, and CTA button text.
105: Score each axis 1-10 using these heuristics:
106: 
107: | Signal | Score direction |
108: |--------|----------------|
109: | Uses "you/your" frequently | formal_casual → casual (+2) |
110: | Uses technical jargon | expert_accessible → expert (-2) |
111: | Short punchy sentences (≤8 words) | bold_subtle → bold (+2) |
112: | Data/stats in hero | rational_emotional → rational (-2) |
113: | "Transform", "revolutionize", "disrupt" | traditional_innovative → innovative (+2) |
114: | Customer testimonials lead | rational_emotional → emotional (+2) |
115: | Industry awards, "trusted by X" | traditional_innovative → traditional (-1) |
116: 
117: ### Confidence Scoring
118: Each voice axis gets a confidence rating based on signal count:
119: - **High** (3+ signals): strong evidence for axis position
120: - **Medium** (2 signals): moderate evidence, may need validation
121: - **Low** (1 signal): weak evidence, treat as estimate
122: 
123: Also extract structured data when available: schema.org markup, Open Graph tags (og:title, og:description, og:image), Twitter Card metadata.
124: 
125: **Imagery style** (from og:image and any visible hero image descriptions):
126: - Photography vs. illustration vs. flat design
127: - Subject matter (people, product, abstract, data)
128: - Composition style (clean/minimal vs. busy/editorial)
129: 
130: **Forbidden elements** (infer from brand positioning):
131: - Enterprise/B2B brands → add "cheesy stock photos", "consumer lifestyle imagery"
132: - Healthcare → add "unqualified medical claims", "before/after imagery"
133: - Finance → add "get rich quick imagery", "unrealistic wealth displays"
134: - Consumer brands → usually no forbidden elements
135: 
136: ### Step 4: Build brand-profile.json
137: 
138: Read `~/.claude/skills/ads/references/brand-dna-template.md` for the exact schema.
139: 
140: Construct the JSON object following the schema precisely. Use `null` for any
141: field that cannot be confidently extracted; do not guess.
142: 
143: Example of a low-confidence field:
144: ```json
145: "typography": {
146:   "heading_font": null,
147:   "body_font": "system-ui",
148:   "pairing_descriptor": "system default (Google Fonts not detected)"
149: }
150: ```
151: 
152: ### Step 5: Write brand-profile.json
153: 
154: Write the JSON to `./brand-profile.json` in the current working directory
155: (where the user is running Claude Code).
156: 
157: If screenshots were captured successfully in Step 2b, include a `screenshots` field:
158: ```json
159: "screenshots": {
160:   "homepage": "./brand-screenshots/{domain}_homepage.png",
161:   "product": "./brand-screenshots/{domain}_product.png",
162:   "about": "./brand-screenshots/{domain}_about.png"
163: }
164: ```
165: Include only the screenshots that were successfully captured. If a page was not
166: found or errored, omit that key. Omit the `screenshots` field entirely if Step 2b
167: was skipped or all captures failed.
168: 
169: ### Step 6: Confirm and Summarize
170: 
171: Show the user:
172: ```
173: ✓ brand-profile.json saved to ./brand-profile.json
174: 
175: Brand DNA Summary:
176:   Brand: [brand_name]
177:   Voice: [descriptor 1], [descriptor 2], [descriptor 3]
178:   Primary Color: [hex]
179:   Typography: [heading_font] / [body_font]
180:   Target: [age_range] [profession]
181:   Screenshots: [N captured (homepage, product, about) in ./brand-screenshots/] OR [skipped]
182: 
183: Run `/ads create` to generate campaign concepts from this profile.
184: ```
185: 
186: ## Visual Designer Integration
187: 
188: The visual-designer agent uses the most relevant screenshot per concept as a style
189: reference when generating images via banana. For example, a product-focused concept
190: references the product page screenshot, while a brand awareness concept references
191: the homepage or about page screenshot.
192: 
193: ## Limitations
194: 
195: - **Sparse content**: Sites with <200 words of body text produce lower-confidence profiles.
196:   Note: "Low confidence extraction; limited content available for analysis."
197: - **Dynamic sites**: JavaScript-rendered content may not be captured. Playwright is not
198:   used by default. If the site appears to be SPA/React with no static HTML, note this.
199: - **Multi-brand enterprises**: This tool creates one profile per URL. Run separately
200:   for each brand/product line.
201: - **Dark mode sites**: If body background is #333 or darker, swap background/text values.
202: - **CSS-in-JS**: Modern React sites may not have extractable CSS. Use og:image colors as fallback.
203: 
204: ## brand-profile.json Schema
205: 
206: ```json
207: {
208:   "schema_version": "1.0",
209:   "brand_name": "string",
210:   "website_url": "string",
211:   "extracted_at": "ISO-8601",
212:   "voice": {
213:     "formal_casual": 1-10,
214:     "rational_emotional": 1-10,
215:     "playful_serious": 1-10,
216:     "bold_subtle": 1-10,
217:     "traditional_innovative": 1-10,
218:     "expert_accessible": 1-10,
219:     "descriptors": ["adjective1", "adjective2", "adjective3"]
220:   },
221:   "colors": {
222:     "primary": "#hexcode or null",
223:     "secondary": ["#hex1", "#hex2"],
224:     "forbidden": ["#hex or color name"],
225:     "background": "#hexcode",
226:     "text": "#hexcode"
227:   },
228:   "typography": {
229:     "heading_font": "Font Name or null",
230:     "body_font": "Font Name or system-ui",
231:     "pairing_descriptor": "brief description"
232:   },
233:   "imagery": {
234:     "style": "professional photography | illustration | flat design | mixed",
235:     "subjects": ["subject1", "subject2"],
236:     "composition": "brief description",
237:     "forbidden": ["element1", "element2"]
238:   },
239:   "aesthetic": {
240:     "mood_keywords": ["keyword1", "keyword2", "keyword3"],
241:     "texture": "minimal | textured | mixed",
242:     "negative_space": "generous | moderate | dense"
243:   },
244:   "brand_values": ["value1", "value2", "value3"],
245:   "target_audience": {
246:     "age_range": "e.g. 25-45",
247:     "profession": "brief description",
248:     "pain_points": ["pain1", "pain2"],
249:     "aspirations": ["aspiration1", "aspiration2"]
250:   }
251: }
252: ```

(End of file - total 252 lines)
</content>

7: ---
8: 
9: # Ads Create: Campaign Concept & Copy Brief Generator
10: 
11: Generates structured campaign concepts and platform-specific copy from your brand
12: profile and optional audit data. Outputs `campaign-brief.md` for use by `/ads generate`.
13: 
14: Operates under the **10-Principle Thinking Framework** (see
15: `ads/references/thinking-framework.md`). LISTEN to the brand voice before
16: typing; FEEL the emotional resonance of every headline and CTA; CREATE
17: the deliverable — do not hedge concepts so heavily that the next person
18: has to do the work. Spec compliance without emotional pull is a fail.
19: 
20: ## Quick Reference
21: 
22: | Command | What it does |
23: |---------|-------------|
24: | `/ads create` | Full campaign brief → `campaign-brief.md` |
25: | `/ads create --platforms meta google` | Brief for specific platforms only |
26: | `/ads create --objective leads` | Brief optimized for lead generation |
27: 
28: ## Process
29: 
30: ### Step 1: Check for Brand Profile
31: 
32: Look for `brand-profile.json` in the current directory.
33: 
34: - **Found**: Load and proceed.
35: - **Not found**: Ask the user:
36:   > "I don't see a brand-profile.json in this directory. Would you like to:
37:   > 1. Run `/ads dna <url>` first to extract brand DNA automatically
38:   > 2. Describe your brand manually (I'll create a basic profile from your description)"
39: 
40: If the user chooses manual, collect:
41: - Brand name and website
42: - Primary color (or "unsure")
43: - 3 words that describe the brand voice
44: - Target audience (age, role, key pain point)
45: - Main product/service offering
46: 
47: ### Step 2: Check for Audit Results
48: 
49: Look for `ADS-AUDIT-REPORT.md` or any `*-audit-results.md` in the current directory.
50: 
51: - **Found**: Read them. Note the top 3 weaknesses (creative fatigue, tracking gaps, wasted spend) to address in concepts.
52: - **Not found**: Continue without. Note in the brief: "No audit data found; concepts are generalized. Run `/ads audit` for weakness-targeted concepts."
53: 
54: ### Step 3: Collect Campaign Parameters
55: 
56: If `--platforms` or `--objective` flags were provided in the command, use those values
57: and skip the corresponding questions below.
58: 
59: Ask (combine into one message; omit any already provided via flags):
60: 1. **Platforms**: Which ad platforms? (Meta · Google · LinkedIn · TikTok · YouTube · Microsoft · All)
61: 2. **Objective**: Sales/Revenue · Leads/Demos · App Installs · Brand Awareness · Retargeting
62: 3. **Offer or brief**: Any specific offer, promotion, or message to highlight? (optional)
63: 4. **Number of concepts**: How many campaign concepts? (default: 3)
64: 
65: ### Step 4: Select Copy Framework
66: 
67: Read `ads/references/copy-frameworks.md` and recommend a framework based on
68: campaign goal + platform + audience temperature:
69: 
70: | Framework | Best For |
71: |-----------|----------|
72: | AIDA (Attention, Interest, Desire, Action) | Cold audiences, awareness campaigns |
73: | PAS (Problem, Agitate, Solve) | Pain-point products, problem-aware audiences |
74: | BAB (Before, After, Bridge) | Transformation offers, coaching, fitness |
75: | 4P (Promise, Picture, Proof, Push) | Direct response, high-intent audiences |
76: | FAB (Features, Advantages, Benefits) | Product-focused, comparison shoppers |
77: | Star-Story-Solution | Brand storytelling, warm audiences |
78: 
79: Include the selected framework name in campaign-brief.md for the copy-writer agent.
80: 
81: ### Step 5: Spawn Creative Agents in Sequence
82: 
83: Agents must run **sequentially**; `copy-writer` reads the file that `creative-strategist`
84: writes, so running them in parallel creates a race condition on `campaign-brief.md`.
85: 
86: **Step 5a; Spawn `creative-strategist`** (Task tool):
87: This agent creates `campaign-brief.md` and writes the strategic sections:
88: `## Brand DNA Summary`, `## Campaign Concepts`, `## Image Generation Briefs`, `## Next Steps`.
89: 
90: Additional instructions for `creative-strategist`:
91: - For e-commerce businesses, also read `skills/ads-plan/assets/ecommerce-creative.md`
92:   and select the appropriate creative playbook (Product Launch, Sale/Promotion,
93:   Seasonal, Retargeting, Brand Awareness)
94: - Include banana domain mode recommendations in each Image Generation Brief
95:   (Product, Editorial, Cinema, UI/Web, or Portrait)
96: 
97: Wait for `creative-strategist` to **fully complete** before continuing.
98: 
99: **Step 5b; Spawn `copy-writer`** (Task tool):
100: After `creative-strategist` completes, spawn `copy-writer`. It reads the existing
101: `campaign-brief.md` and appends the `## Copy Deck` section with platform-specific
102: headlines, primary text, and CTAs.
103: 
104: Additional instructions for `copy-writer`:
105: - Read `ads/references/copy-frameworks.md` and apply the selected framework
106:   structure to all ad copy
107: - Generate 2 framework variants per platform: primary (recommended framework)
108:   + secondary (alternative for A/B testing)
109: 
110: Wait for `copy-writer` to complete before proceeding to Step 6.
111: 
112: ### Step 6: Review and Present
113: 
114: After both agents complete, confirm `campaign-brief.md` exists and is complete.
115: 
116: Present a summary to the user:
117: ```
118: ✓ campaign-brief.md generated
119: 
120: Summary:
121:   Concepts: [N] campaign concepts created
122:   Platforms: [list]
123:   Copy deck: Headlines, primary text, and CTAs for each concept × platform
124:   Image briefs: [N] image generation briefs ready
125: 
126: Next steps:
127:   1. Review campaign-brief.md and adjust any messaging
128:   2. Run `/ads generate` to produce AI images from the briefs
129:   3. Upload copy and assets to your ad platforms
130: ```
131: 
132: ## campaign-brief.md Format Specification
133: 
134: The following section headings are a **parsing contract**; agents downstream depend on these exact heading names.
135: 
136: ```markdown
137: # Campaign Brief: [brand_name]
138: **Generated:** [date]
139: **Website:** [website_url]
140: **Platforms:** [comma-separated list]
141: **Objective:** [objective]
142: **Concepts:** [N]
143: 
144: ## Brand DNA Summary
145: [3-sentence synthesis of brand-profile.json: voice, visual identity, target audience]
146: 
147: ## Audit Context
148: [If audit data found: top 3 weaknesses being addressed]
149: [If no audit data: "No audit data; run /ads audit for weakness-targeted concepts"]
150: 
151: ## Campaign Concepts
152: 
153: ### Concept 1: [Name]
154: **Hypothesis:** [why this will work; 1 sentence]
155: **Primary Message:** [core message; 1 sentence]
156: **Tone:** [voice reading from brand-profile.json]
157: **Visual Direction:** [2-3 sentences describing imagery]
158: **Target Platforms:** [platforms and rationale]
159: **CTA:** [call to action text]
160: **Addresses:** [audit finding or "general brand awareness"]
161: 
162: ### Concept 2: [Name]
163: [same structure]
164: 
165: [repeat for all concepts]
166: 
167: ## Copy Deck
168: [appended by copy-writer agent; headlines, primary text, CTAs per concept per platform]
169: 
170: ## Image Generation Briefs
171: 
172: ### Brief 1: [Concept Name]: [Platform]
173: **Prompt:** [exact generation prompt]
174: **Dimensions:** [WxH]
175: **Safe zone notes:** [constraint or "None"]
176: 
177: ### Brief 2: [Concept Name]: [Platform]
178: **Prompt:** [exact generation prompt]
179: **Dimensions:** [WxH]
180: **Safe zone notes:** [constraint or "None"]
181: 
182: [one brief per concept × platform combination]
183: 
184: ## Next Steps
185: 1. Review all concepts and select which to move forward with
186: 2. Run `/ads generate` to produce images from the briefs above
187: 3. Adjust CTAs and offers in the copy deck for your specific promotion
188: 4. Upload final assets to your ad platform managers
189: ```
190: 
191: ## Quality Gates
192: 
193: - **Minimum 3 concepts** (unless user requests fewer)
194: - **Distinct angles**: no two concepts share the same primary message angle
195: - **Platform fit**: concepts targeting TikTok must acknowledge vertical-only format and sound-on context
196: - **Offer anchoring**: if the user provided a specific offer, at least 1 concept must lead with it
197: - **Image briefs**: every concept must have at least one image brief per requested platform
198: 
199: ## Meta Andromeda Optimization
200: 
201: For Meta campaigns: recommend diverse creative concepts (different motivators, visual styles, messaging angles) to maximize Andromeda retrieval. Similarity Score >60% between ads triggers clustering and suppression.
202: 
203: ## Platform Character Limits
204: 
205: Verify platform character limits are current. Meta reduced headline display length on mobile in 2025.

(End of file - total 205 lines)
</content>

# Ads Create: Campaign Concept & Copy Brief Generator

Generates structured campaign concepts and platform-specific copy from your brand
profile and optional audit data. Outputs `campaign-brief.md` for use by `/ads generate`.

Operates under the **10-Principle Thinking Framework** (see
`ads/references/thinking-framework.md`). LISTEN to the brand voice before
typing; FEEL the emotional resonance of every headline and CTA; CREATE
the deliverable — do not hedge concepts so heavily that the next person
has to do the work. Spec compliance without emotional pull is a fail.

## Quick Reference

7: ---
8: 
9: # Ads Generate: AI Ad Image Generator
10: 
11: Generates platform-sized ad creative images from your campaign brief and brand
12: profile. Uses banana-claude as the image generation provider.
13: 
14: ## Quick Reference
15: 
16: | Command | What it does |
17: |---------|-------------|
18: | `/ads generate` | Generate all images from campaign-brief.md |
19: | `/ads generate --platform meta` | Generate Meta assets only |
20: | `/ads generate --prompt "text" --ratio 9:16` | Standalone generation without brief |
21: 
22: ## Environment Setup
23: 
24: **Required before running:**
25: 
26: - Requires banana-claude (v1.4.1+) with nanobanana-mcp configured
27: - Run `/banana setup` to configure API key and MCP
28: - Fallback: if banana is not available, use `scripts/generate_image.py` (deprecated)
29: 
30: If banana-claude is not installed, this skill will display setup instructions
31: and stop. It will never fail silently.
32: 
33: If banana-claude is unavailable, alternatives include: OpenAI gpt-image-1 ($0.040/image), Stability SD 3.5 ($0.065), or Replicate FLUX.1 Pro ($0.055). Configure via ADS_IMAGE_PROVIDER env var.
34: 
35: ## Process
36: 
37: ### Step 1: Verify banana-claude
38: 
39: Verify banana-claude is installed (run `/banana setup` to check). If not installed,
40: display setup instructions and exit.
41: 
42: ### Step 2: Locate Source Files
43: 
44: Check for:
45: - `campaign-brief.md` → primary source for prompts and dimensions
46: - `brand-profile.json` → brand color/style injection (optional but recommended)
47: 
48: **If campaign-brief.md is found**: Use `## Image Generation Briefs` section as the
49: generation job list.
50: 
51: **If no campaign-brief.md**: Enter standalone mode (Step 2b).
52: 
53: #### Step 2b: Standalone Mode
54: 
55: Ask the user:
56: 1. Generation prompt (what should the image show?)
57: 2. Target platform (to set correct dimensions)
58: 3. Output filename (optional)
59: 
60: Then skip to Step 5.
61: 
62: ### Step 3: Read Provider Config
63: 
64: Load `~/.claude/skills/ads/references/image-providers.md` to confirm:
65: - Active provider pricing (show user the cost estimate)
66: - Rate limits for current tier
67: - Batch API availability
68: 
69: ### Step 4: Read Platform Specs
70: 
71: For each platform in the campaign brief, load the relevant spec reference:
72: - `~/.claude/skills/ads/references/meta-creative-specs.md`
73: - `~/.claude/skills/ads/references/google-creative-specs.md`
74: - `~/.claude/skills/ads/references/tiktok-creative-specs.md`
75: - `~/.claude/skills/ads/references/linkedin-creative-specs.md`
76: - `~/.claude/skills/ads/references/youtube-creative-specs.md`
77: - `~/.claude/skills/ads/references/microsoft-creative-specs.md`
78: 
79: ### Step 5: Prepare banana Configuration
80: 
81: Create banana brand preset from brand-profile.json if one does not already exist
82: at `~/.banana/presets/{brand-slug}.json`.
83: 
84: Select banana domain mode based on campaign brief content:
85: - **Product**: e-commerce, packshots
86: - **Editorial**: brand awareness, lifestyle
87: - **Cinema**: video thumbnails, dramatic
88: - **UI/Web**: app install, SaaS
89: - **Portrait**: testimonials, people
90: 
91: ### Step 6: Spawn Visual Designer Agent
92: 
93: Spawn the `visual-designer` agent using the Task tool with `context: fork`,
94: passing the selected domain mode and preset name.
95: 
96: The agent will:
97: - Parse the image generation briefs from campaign-brief.md
98: - Inject brand colors and mood from brand-profile.json
99: - Use banana-claude with the configured domain mode for each asset
100: - Save to `./ad-assets/[platform]/[concept]/` directory structure
101: - Write `generation-manifest.json`
102: 
103: ### Step 7: Validate with Format Adapter
104: 
105: After the visual-designer completes, spawn the `format-adapter` agent
106: with `context: fork` to validate dimensions and report missing formats.
107: 
108: ### Step 8: Quality Gate
109: 
110: Use Claude vision to assess each generated image against the brief (score 1 to 10
111: on brand alignment, composition, platform fit). If any image scores below 6,
112: regenerate once with an adjusted prompt.
113: 
114: Quality Gate Rubric:
115: - 9-10: Professional quality, brand-aligned, platform-optimized, no issues
116: - 7-8: Good quality, minor composition or brand alignment improvements possible
117: - 5-6: Acceptable but needs regeneration. Text readability issues, poor composition, or brand mismatch
118: - Below 5: Reject. Regenerate with adjusted prompt
119: 
120: ### Step 9: Aggregate Costs
121: 
122: Read banana cost data from `~/.banana/costs.json` and include total creative spend
123: in generation-manifest.json.
124: 
125: ### Step 10: Report Results
126: 
127: Present a summary:
128: ```
129: Generation complete:
130: 
131:   Generated assets:
132:     ✓ ./ad-assets/meta/concept-1/feed-1080x1350.png
133:     ✓ ./ad-assets/tiktok/concept-1/vertical-1080x1920.png
134:     ✗ ./ad-assets/google/concept-1/landscape-1200x628.png [error reason]
135: 
136:   Format validation: See format-report.md
137: 
138:   Cost: $[N] total creative spend (from ~/.banana/costs.json)
139: 
140:   Next steps:
141:     1. Review assets in ./ad-assets/
142:     2. Check format-report.md for any missing formats
143:     3. Upload to your ad platform managers
144: ```
145: 
146: ## Cost Transparency
147: 
148: Before generating, estimate and show the cost:
149: - Count the number of image briefs in campaign-brief.md
150: - Show estimated cost based on banana pricing tiers
151: - If >$1.00, ask for confirmation before proceeding
152: 
153: ## Standalone Mode (No campaign-brief.md)
154: 
155: When running without a campaign brief:
156: 
157: ```
158: Platform target → dimensions used:
159:   meta-feed     → 1080×1350 (4:5)
160:   meta-reels    → 1080×1920 (9:16)
161:   tiktok        → 1080×1920 (9:16)
162:   google-pmax   → 1200×628 (1.91:1)
163:   linkedin      → 1080×1080 (1:1)
164:   youtube       → 1280×720 (16:9)
165:   youtube-short → 1080×1920 (9:16)
166: ```
167: 
168: Use `/banana generate` directly with the specified prompt and aspect ratio.
169: 
170: ## Reference Files
171: 
172: - `~/.claude/skills/ads/references/image-providers.md`: provider config, pricing, limits
173: - `~/.claude/skills/ads/references/[platform]-creative-specs.md`: per-platform specs
174: - `~/.claude/skills/ads/references/brand-dna-template.md`: brand injection schema

(End of file - total 174 lines)

# Ads Generate: AI Ad Image Generator

Generates platform-sized ad creative images from your campaign brief and brand
profile. Uses banana-claude as the image generation provider.

## Quick Reference

| Command | What it does |
|---------|-------------|
| `/ads generate` | Generate all images from campaign-brief.md |
| `/ads generate --platform meta` | Generate Meta assets only |
| `/ads generate --prompt "text" --ratio 9:16` | Standalone generation without brief |

1: ---
2: name: ads-photoshoot
3: description: "Product photography enhancement for ad creatives using banana-claude image generation. Takes a product image and generates 5 professional photography styles for ad use: Studio, Floating, Ingredient, In Use, and Lifestyle. Requires banana-claude (v1.4.1+) with nanobanana-mcp. Triggers on: product photo, product photography, photoshoot, enhance product image, product shoot, product photos for ads, generate product photos, studio shot, lifestyle photo."
4: user-invokable: false
5: tested_date: 2026-05-17
6: tested_with: claude-code v2.x
7: ---
8: 
9: # Ads Photoshoot: AI Product Photography
10: 
11: Transforms a product image or description into professional ad-ready photography
12: in 5 distinct visual styles. Each style generates at two sizes: 1:1 (Meta/LinkedIn)
13: and 9:16 (TikTok/Reels/Stories).
14: 
15: ## Quick Reference
16: 
17: | Command | What it does |
18: |---------|-------------|
19: | `/ads photoshoot` | Interactive: ask for product + styles |
20: | `/ads photoshoot --styles studio floating` | Generate only selected styles |
21: | `/ads photoshoot --product shoe.jpg` | Start with a product image file |
22: | `/ads photoshoot --all-platforms` | Generate all 5 sizes per style |
23: 
24: ## Environment Setup
25: 
26: Requires banana-claude (v1.4.1+) with nanobanana-mcp configured.
27: Run `/banana setup` to configure API key and MCP.
28: 
29: ## Process
30: 
31: ### Step 1: Collect Product Information
32: 
33: Ask (combine into one message):
34: 1. **Product image**: Path to product image file (local) OR product URL OR text description
35:    > "Provide a product image path (e.g. ./product.jpg), a URL, or describe your product"
36: 2. **Product description**: What is it? Key features to highlight? (helps prompt quality)
37: 3. **Styles to generate**: Which of the 5 styles? (default: all 5)
38:    - Studio, Floating, Ingredient, In Use, Lifestyle
39: 4. **Target platforms**: Which platforms will these run on?
40:    - Determines output sizes (default: Meta + TikTok → 1:1 + 9:16)
41: 
42: ### Step 2: Load Brand Profile (Optional)
43: 
44: Check for `brand-profile.json` in the current directory.
45: 
46: If found, extract for style injection:
47: - `colors.primary` → inject into backgrounds and accent elements
48: - `aesthetic.mood_keywords` → inject as atmosphere descriptors
49: - `target_audience` → use for Lifestyle and In Use context
50: - `imagery.forbidden` → exclude from all prompts
51: 
52: If not found, proceed with standard style templates.
53: 
54: ### Step 3: Verify banana-claude
55: 
56: Verify banana-claude is installed (run `/banana setup` to check). If not installed,
57: display setup instructions and exit.
58: 
59: ### Step 4: Construct Prompts per Style
60: 
61: For each selected style, build the prompt using the template + product description + brand DNA.
62: 
63: #### Style 1: Studio
64: Clean, e-commerce style product shot.
65: 
66: **Base template:**
67: ```
68: "[product description], professional product photography, clean white seamless
69: background, even studio lighting, soft drop shadow, high detail product focus,
70: ecommerce style, [brand.colors.primary] subtle accent reflections if applicable,
71: top-down or 3/4 angle, no distractions, catalog quality"
72: ```
73: 
74: **Composition:** Centered, slight 3/4 angle or flat lay.
75: **Output sizes:** 1080×1080, 1080×1920
76: 
77: #### Style 2: Floating
78: Dramatic levitation effect.
79: 
80: **Base template:**
81: ```
82: "[product description] floating in mid-air, dramatic floating product shot,
83: [brand.colors.primary or brand.aesthetic.mood_keywords[0]] gradient background,
84: atmospheric shadow below product, levitation effect, product defying gravity,
85: clean modern aesthetic, high contrast, striking visual"
86: ```
87: 
88: **Composition:** Product centered vertically, ample space above and below.
89: **Output sizes:** 1080×1080, 1080×1920
90: 
91: #### Style 3: Ingredient
92: Flat lay with components.
93: 
94: **Base template:**
95: ```
96: "[product description] centered flat lay, surrounded by its key ingredients
97: or materials artfully arranged, top-down overhead view, clean light background,
98: natural texture surface, product as hero element, ingredients scattered with
99: intentional negative space, editorial food photography style"
100: ```
101: 
102: **Composition:** Top-down, product in center, ingredients fanning out.
103: **Output sizes:** 1080×1080 (optimal for this style). Also generate 9:16 vertical for TikTok/Reels/Stories placements.
104: 
105: #### Style 4: In Use
106: Authentic usage context.
107: 
108: **Base template:**
109: ```
110: "person's hands using [product description] in natural context, lifestyle
111: photography, focus on product-hand interaction, shallow depth of field,
112: warm natural window light, authentic not staged, [brand.target_audience.profession]
113: implied context, [brand.aesthetic.mood_keywords] atmosphere"
114: ```
115: 
116: **Composition:** Hands prominent, product clearly identifiable, background soft-focus.
117: **Note:** Hands only; no full face (avoids model release complications).
118: **Output sizes:** 1080×1080, 1080×1920
119: 
120: #### Style 5: Lifestyle
121: Aspirational full-context shot.
122: 
123: **Base template:**
124: ```
125: "[product description] in aspirational lifestyle scene, [brand.target_audience.age_range]
126: demographic implied environment, [brand.target_audience.profession] context,
127: [brand.aesthetic.mood_keywords] atmosphere, golden hour or clean natural lighting,
128: editorial photography style, [brand.aesthetic.negative_space] composition,
129: product clearly visible and prominent"
130: ```
131: 
132: **Composition:** Environmental context, product as hero element within the scene.
133: **Output sizes:** 1080×1080, 1080×1920
134: 
135: ### Iterative Refinement
136: 
137: For iterative refinement: if initial generation doesn't match brand expectations, adjust the prompt by specifying: lighting direction, color temperature, background texture, or product angle before regenerating.
138: 
139: ### Step 5: Generate Images
140: 
141: **Domain mode selection per style:**
142: - Use banana **Product** mode for Studio, Floating, and Ingredient styles
143: - Use banana **Editorial** mode for In Use and Lifestyle styles
144: - Set resolution to 2K (default) for all generations
145: 
146: **Aspect ratio setup:** Use banana MCP `set_aspect_ratio` before each generation:
147: - For 1080x1080: set ratio to 1:1
148: - For 1080x1920: set ratio to 9:16
149: 
150: For each style x size combination, use `/banana generate` with the constructed
151: prompt, selected domain mode, and correct aspect ratio. Save output to
152: `./product-photos/[style]/[product-slug]-[style]-[WxH].png`.
153: 
154: Track results. If a generation fails, retry once with a simplified prompt.
155: 
156: ### Step 6: Organize and Report
157: 
158: **Output directory structure:**
159: ```
160: ./product-photos/
161:   studio/
162:     product-studio-1080x1080.png
163:     product-studio-1080x1920.png
164:   floating/
165:     product-floating-1080x1080.png
166:     product-floating-1080x1920.png
167:   ingredient/
168:     product-ingredient-1080x1080.png
169:     product-ingredient-1080x1920.png
170:   in-use/
171:     product-in-use-1080x1080.png
172:     product-in-use-1080x1920.png
173:   lifestyle/
174:     product-lifestyle-1080x1080.png
175:     product-lifestyle-1080x1920.png
176: ```
177: 
178: **Summary:**
179: ```
180: ✓ Product photos generated: [N] images
181: 
182:   Studio:     ./product-photos/studio/ (2 sizes)
183:   Floating:   ./product-photos/floating/ (2 sizes)
184:   Ingredient: ./product-photos/ingredient/ (2 sizes)
185:   In Use:     ./product-photos/in-use/ (2 sizes)
186:   Lifestyle:  ./product-photos/lifestyle/ (2 sizes)
187: 
188:   Cost: see ~/.banana/costs.json for total spend
189: 
190:   Best for:
191:   • Meta Feed → Studio (4:5) or Lifestyle (4:5)
192:   • TikTok/Reels → Floating (9:16) or In Use (9:16)
193:   • LinkedIn → Studio (1:1) or Lifestyle (1:1)
194:   • Google PMax → Studio (1:1); crop to 1.91:1 after
195: 
196:   Run `/ads generate` to use these in a full campaign.
197: ```
198: 
199: ## Cost Estimate
200: 
201: Before generating, show:
202: - Number of styles selected x 2 sizes = total images
203: - Estimated cost based on banana pricing tiers
204: - If >$0.50, ask for confirmation
205: 
206: ## Platform Recommendations
207: 
208: | Style | Best Platforms | Rationale |
209: |-------|---------------|-----------|
210: | Studio | Meta Feed, LinkedIn, Google PMax | Universal, clean, platform-safe |
211: | Floating | Meta Reels, TikTok, Stories | High visual impact on vertical placements |
212: | Ingredient | Meta Feed, Pinterest | Works best as square; tells product story |
213: | In Use | TikTok, Meta Reels, Stories | Authentic, native-feeling content |
214: | Lifestyle | All platforms | Aspirational, broad audience appeal |
215: 
216: ## Reference Files
217: 
218: - `~/.claude/skills/ads/references/image-providers.md`: API setup and pricing
219: - `~/.claude/skills/ads/references/brand-dna-template.md`: Brand injection schema
220: - `~/.claude/skills/ads/references/meta-creative-specs.md`: Safe zone for 9:16
221: - `~/.claude/skills/ads/references/tiktok-creative-specs.md`: Safe zone constraints

(End of file - total 221 lines)
</content>

869: def _assert_router_mounted(router_name: str) -> None:
870:     """Assert that a critical router is mounted. Fails startup if not found."""
871:     from fastapi import routing
872:     mounted_routes = [route.path for route in app.routes]
873:     
874:     # Check for router-specific paths
875:     router_path_indicators = {
876:         "subscription": ["/api/subscription/plans", "/api/subscription/preflight"],
877:         "podcast": ["/api/podcast/projects", "/api/podcast/"],
878:         "blog_writer": ["/api/blog/health", "/api/blog/research/start"],
879:     }
880:     
881:     expected_paths = router_path_indicators.get(router_name, [])
882:     found = any(path in mounted_routes for path in expected_paths)
883:     
884:     if found:
885:         logger.info(f"✅ Critical router '{router_name}' is mounted")
886:     else:
887:         error_msg = f"❌ CRITICAL: Router '{router_name}' is NOT mounted! Expected paths: {expected_paths}"
888:         logger.error(error_msg)
889:         # In feature-only mode, only fail if the feature is expected
890:         if not _is_full_mode() and _is_feature_enabled(router_name):
891:             raise RuntimeError(error_msg)

1: #!/usr/bin/env python3
2: """
3: ALwrity Backend Server - Modular Startup Script
4: Handles setup, dependency installation, and server startup using modular utilities.
5: Run this from the backend directory to set up and start the FastAPI server.
6: """
7: 
8: import os
9: import sys
10: import json
11: import argparse
12: import platform
13: from pathlib import Path
14: from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
15: from typing import Optional
16: 
17: # Detect platform
18: IS_WINDOWS = platform.system() == "Windows"
19: IS_LINUX = platform.system() == "Linux"
20: 
21: import uvicorn
22: 
23: 
24: @dataclass
25: class BootstrapResult:
26:     name: str
27:     success: bool
28:     skipped: bool
29:     reason: Optional[str] = None
30:     details: Optional[str] = None
31: 
32: 
33: LINGUISTIC_REQUIRED_FEATURES = {"content_planning", "strategy_copilot", "facebook", "linkedin", "blog_writer", "persona"}
34: 
35: 
36: def get_enabled_features() -> set:
37:     """Get enabled features from ALWRITY_ENABLED_FEATURES env var.
38:     
39:     Values:
40:     - "all" - enable all features (default)
41:     - comma-separated: "podcast,blog-writer,youtube"
42:     - single feature: "podcast"
43:     """
44:     env_value = os.getenv("ALWRITY_ENABLED_FEATURES", "all").strip().lower()
45:     
46:     if not env_value or env_value == "all":
47:         return {"all"}
48:     
49:     return {f.strip() for f in env_value.split(",") if f.strip()}
50: 
51: 
52: def should_bootstrap_linguistic_models() -> bool:
53:     """Decide whether to bootstrap linguistic models based on enabled features."""
54:     enabled_features = get_enabled_features()
55:     verbose = os.getenv("ALWRITY_VERBOSE", "false").lower() == "true"
56:     
57:     if "all" in enabled_features:
58:         return True
59:     
60:     # Podcast-only mode doesn't need linguistic models
61:     if enabled_features == {"podcast"}:
62:         return False
63:     
64:     # Map old profile names to features for backwards compatibility
65:     feature_mapping = {
66:         "podcast": "podcast",
67:         "youtube": "youtube",
68:         "planning": "content-planning",
69:         "default": "all"
70:     }
71:     
72:     # Check if any linguistic-required feature is enabled
73:     linguistic_features = {"content_planning", "facebook", "linkedin", "blog_writer", "persona"}
74:     return bool(enabled_features & linguistic_features)
75: 
76: 
77: def should_bootstrap_local_llm_models() -> bool:
78:     """Decide whether to bootstrap local LLM models based on enabled features.
79:     
80:     SIF/Story Writer requires local LLM - skip if only podcast is enabled.
81:     """
82:     enabled_features = get_enabled_features()
83:     
84:     if "all" in enabled_features:
85:         return True
86:     
87:     # SIF/Story Writer requires local LLM - only bootstrap if explicitly needed
88:     # Skip for lean deployments (podcast-only, content-planning only, etc.)
89:     return False  # Default to skip unless "all" is enabled
90: 
91: 
92: def bootstrap_linguistic_models() -> BootstrapResult:
93:     """
94:     Bootstrap spaCy and NLTK models BEFORE any imports.
95:     This prevents import-time failures when EnhancedLinguisticAnalyzer is loaded.
96:     """
97:     import subprocess
98:     import os
99:     
100:     verbose = os.getenv("ALWRITY_VERBOSE", "false").lower() == "true"
101:     
102:     if verbose:
103:         print("[DEBUG] Bootstrapping linguistic models...")
104:     
105:     # Check and download spaCy model
106:     try:
107:         import spacy
108:         try:
109:             nlp = spacy.load("en_core_web_sm")
110:             if verbose:
111:                 print("   [OK] spaCy model 'en_core_web_sm' available")
112:         except OSError:
113:             if verbose:

287: def start_backend(enable_reload=False, production_mode=False):
288:     """Start the backend server."""
289:     print("==> Starting ALwrity Backend...")
290:     # Check for legacy podcast-only demo mode env vars (backward compat)
291:     is_legacy_podcast_mode = os.getenv("ALWRITY_PODCAST_ONLY_DEMO_MODE", os.getenv("PODCAST_ONLY_DEMO_MODE", "false")).lower() in {"1", "true", "yes", "on"}
292:     enabled = get_enabled_features()
293:     is_feature_limited = "all" not in enabled
294: 
295:     if is_legacy_podcast_mode or is_feature_limited:
296:         mode_label = "legacy podcast-only" if is_legacy_podcast_mode else f"feature-limited ({', '.join(sorted(enabled))})"
297:         print(f"\n{'=' * 60}")
298:         print(f"==> {mode_label.upper()} MODE ACTIVE")
299:         print("   Non-matching router groups are intentionally skipped.")
300:         print("=" * 60)
301:     
302:     # Set host based on environment and mode
303:     # Use 127.0.0.1 for local production testing on Windows
304:     # Use 0.0.0.0 for actual cloud deployments (Render, Railway, etc.)
305:     # Render provides PORT env var, detect cloud by presence of PORT
306:     render_port = os.getenv("PORT")
307:     if render_port:
308:         # Cloud deployment detected (Render sets PORT env var) - use 0.0.0.0
309:         os.environ.setdefault("HOST", "0.0.0.0")
310:         os.environ.setdefault("PORT", render_port)
311:     else:
312:         # Local deployment - use 127.0.0.1 for better Windows compatibility
313:         os.environ.setdefault("HOST", "127.0.0.1")
314:     
315:     # Render sets PORT automatically. We should respect it if present, otherwise default to 8000.
316:     # We don't setdefault("PORT", "8000") here because we want to use os.getenv("PORT") directly later
317:     # to catch if it's missing and THEN default.
318:     
319:     # Set reload based on argument or environment variable
320:     if enable_reload and not production_mode:
321:         os.environ.setdefault("RELOAD", "true")
322:         print("   [DEV] Development mode: Auto-reload enabled")
323:     else:
324:         os.environ.setdefault("RELOAD", "false")
325:         print("   [PROD] Production mode: Auto-reload disabled")
326:     
327:     host = os.getenv("HOST", "0.0.0.0")
328:     port = int(os.getenv("PORT", "8000"))
329:     reload = os.environ.get("RELOAD", "false").lower() == "true"
330:     print(f"[DEBUG] Bind prepared - host={host}, port={port}, reload={reload}", flush=True)
331:     print(f"[DEBUG] ENV check - ALWRITY_ENABLED_FEATURES={os.getenv('ALWRITY_ENABLED_FEATURES')}", flush=True)
332:     
333:     print(f"   ==> Host: {host}", flush=True)
334:     print(f"   ==> Port: {port}", flush=True)
335:     print(f"   [DEV] Reload: {reload}", flush=True)
336:     print(f"[DEBUG] About to import app module...", flush=True)
337:     print("[DEBUG] >>> START APP IMPORT <<<", flush=True)
338:     
339:     try:
340:         # Import and run the app
341:         from app import app
342:         print("[DEBUG] >>> END APP IMPORT <<<", flush=True)
343:         
344:         import uvicorn
345:         print(f"[DEBUG] Imported app and uvicorn successfully", flush=True)
346: 
347:         # Note: Database already initialized by DatabaseSetup in main()
348:         
349:         print("\n[WORLD] ALwrity Backend Server", flush=True)
350:         print("=" * 50, flush=True)
351:         print(f"   📖 API Documentation: http://localhost:{os.getenv('PORT', '8000')}/api/docs", flush=True)
352:         print(f"   🔍 Health Check: http://localhost:{os.getenv('PORT', '8000')}/health", flush=True)
353:         print(f"   📊 ReDoc: http://localhost:{os.getenv('PORT', '8000')}/api/redoc", flush=True)
354:         
355:         if not production_mode:
356:             print(f"   📈 API Monitoring: http://localhost:{os.getenv('PORT', '8000')}/api/content-planning/monitoring/health", flush=True)
357:             print(f"   💳 Billing Dashboard: http://localhost:{os.getenv('PORT', '8000')}/api/subscription/plans", flush=True)
358:             print(f"   📊 Usage Tracking: http://localhost:{os.getenv('PORT', '8000')}/api/subscription/usage/demo", flush=True)
359:         
360:         print("\n[STOP]  Press Ctrl+C to stop the server", flush=True)
361:         print("=" * 50, flush=True)
362:         
363:         # Set up clean logging for end users
364:         from logging_config import setup_clean_logging, get_uvicorn_log_level
365:         # Video stack preflight (diagnostics + version assert)
366:         try:
367:             from services.story_writer.video_preflight import (
368:                 log_video_stack_diagnostics,
369:                 assert_supported_moviepy,
370:             )
371:         except Exception:
372:             # Preflight is optional; continue if module missing
373:             log_video_stack_diagnostics = None
374:             assert_supported_moviepy = None
375:         
376:         verbose_mode = setup_clean_logging()
377:         uvicorn_log_level = get_uvicorn_log_level()
378: 
379:         # Log diagnostics and assert versions (fail fast if misconfigured)
380:         try:
381:             if log_video_stack_diagnostics:
382:                 log_video_stack_diagnostics()
383:             if assert_supported_moviepy:
384:                 assert_supported_moviepy()
385:         except Exception as _video_stack_err:
386:             print(f"[ERROR] Video stack preflight failed: {_video_stack_err}")
387:             return False
388:         
389:         print(f"[DEBUG] Starting uvicorn with host={host} port={port}", flush=True)
390:         print("[DEBUG] >>> ABOUT TO CALL UVICORN.RUN() <<<", flush=True)
391:         
392:         # Skip video preflight in feature-limited mode to save memory/time
393:         is_feature_limited = os.getenv("ALWRITY_ENABLED_FEATURES", "").strip().lower() not in ("", "all")
394:         print(f"[DEBUG] Feature-limited mode check: {is_feature_limited}", flush=True)
395:         
396:         if is_feature_limited:
397:             print("[DEBUG] Feature-limited mode - skipping video preflight", flush=True)
398:         else:
399:             # Log diagnostics and assert versions (fail fast if misconfigured)
400:             try:
401:                 if log_video_stack_diagnostics:
402:                     log_video_stack_diagnostics()
403:                 if assert_supported_moviepy:
404:                     assert_supported_moviepy()
405:             except Exception as _video_stack_err:
406:                 print(f"[ERROR] Video stack preflight failed: {_video_stack_err}")
407:                 return False
408:         
409:         uvicorn.run(
410:             "app:app",
411:             host=host,
412:             port=port,
413:             reload=reload,
414:             reload_dirs=["."],  # Strictly watch backend directory only
415:             reload_excludes=[
416:                 "workspace/**/*",
417:                 "*.pyc",
418:                 "*.pyo", 
419:                 "*.pyd",
420:                 "__pycache__",
421:                 "*.log",
422:                 "*.sqlite",
423:                 "*.db",
424:                 "*.tmp",
425:                 "*.temp",
426:                 "test_*.py",
427:                 "temp_*.py",
428:                 "monitoring_data_service.py",
429:                 "test_monitoring_save.py",
430:                 "*.json",
431:                 "*.yaml",
432:                 "*.yml",
433:                 ".env*",
434:                 "logs/**/*",
435:                 "logs",
436:                 "**/*.jsonl",
437:                 "**/*.log",
438:                 "cache/**/*",
439:                 "tmp/**/*",
440:                 "temp/**/*",
441:                 "middleware/*",
442:                 "models/*",
443:                 "scripts/*",
444:                 "alwrity_utils/*"
445:             ],
446:             log_level=uvicorn_log_level
447:         )
448:         print("[DEBUG] uvicorn.run() has finished", flush=True)
449:         
450:     except KeyboardInterrupt:
451:         print("\n\n🛑 Backend stopped by user")
452:     except Exception as e:
453:         print(f"\n[ERROR] Error starting backend: {e}", flush=True)
454:         import traceback
455:         traceback.print_exc()
456:         return False
457:     
458:     return True

460: 
461: def main():
462:     """Main function to set up and start the backend."""
463:     # Parse command line arguments
464:     parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="ALwrity Backend Server")
465:     parser.add_argument("--reload", action="store_true", help="Enable auto-reload for development")
466:     parser.add_argument("--dev", action="store_true", help="Enable development mode (auto-reload)")
467:     parser.add_argument("--production", action="store_true", help="Enable production mode (optimized for deployment)")
468:     parser.add_argument("--verbose", action="store_true", help="Enable verbose logging for debugging")

472:     production_mode = args.production

474:     verbose_mode = args.verbose

479:     print("[*] ALwrity Backend Server")

481:     print(f"Mode: {'PRODUCTION' if production_mode else 'DEVELOPMENT'}")

483:     if verbose_mode:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
ALwrity Backend Server - Modular Startup Script
Handles setup, dependency installation, and server startup using modular utilities.
Run this from the backend directory to set up and start the FastAPI server.
"""

import os
import sys
import json
import argparse
import platform
from pathlib import Path
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Optional

# Detect platform
IS_WINDOWS = platform.system() == "Windows"
IS_LINUX = platform.system() == "Linux"
